title: Volumes of Revolution: Disks and Washers

epigraph.text:
He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also 
set eternity in the human heart; yet no one can fathom what God has done from 
beginning to end.

content.text:
Spin a curve around an axis and you get a three-dimensional solid. A 
semicircle spun around its diameter produces a sphere. A right triangle 
spun around a leg produces a cone. A rectangle spun around one side 
produces a cylinder. These *solids of revolution* arise everywhere in 
engineering—pistons, columns, bottles, lamp shades, domes—and calculus 
gives us the tools to find their volumes exactly.

The strategy is the same as always: **Slice, Approximate, Sum, 
Integrate.**
The Disk Method

Consider the region under $y = f(x) \ge 0$ from $x = a$ to $x = b$, 
revolved about the $x$-axis. The resulting solid is filled—no hole in 
the middle.

**Step 1: Slice.** Cut the solid perpendicular to the axis of 
revolution (here, perpendicular to the $x$-axis). Each slice is a thin 
*disk*—a circular cylinder of thickness $\Delta x$.

**Step 2: Approximate.** At position $x_k^*$, the disk has radius 
$r = f(x_k^*)$ and thickness $\Delta x$. Its volume is
\[
 \Delta V_k \approx \pi\bigl[f(x_k^*)\bigr]^2 \,\Delta x.
\]

**Step 3: Sum.**
\[
 V \approx \sum_{k=1}^{n} \pi\bigl[f(x_k^*)\bigr]^2\,\Delta x.
\]
**Step 4: Take the limit.**

content.title: Volume by Disks (Revolution about the $x$-axis)
content.content:
If $f$ is continuous and nonneg on $[a,b]$, the volume of the solid 
obtained by revolving the region under $y = f(x)$ about the $x$-axis is
\[
 V = \pi\defint{a}{b} \bigl[f(x)\bigr]^2\dx.
\]

content.caption: The disk method for volumes of revolution
content.alt: Diagram showing the disk method for computing volume of a solid of revolution. On the left, a curve y = f(x) is shown above the x-axis from x = a to x = b, with a thin vertical strip of width Δx highlighted. On the right, the resulting three-dimensional solid is shown after revolving the region about the x-axis. A representative disk-shaped cross-section is highlighted, with radius r = f(x) and thickness Δx. The disk has volume πr²Δx.

content.text:
Think of it this way: every cross-sectional slice perpendicular to the axis 
is a circle of area $\pi r^2 = \pi[f(x)]^2$. The integral sums these 
circular cross-sections across the length of the solid.

content.title: Volume of a cone
content.problem:
Find the volume of the cone obtained by revolving the line $y = \dfrac{r}{h}\,x$ 
about the $x$-axis, from $x = 0$ to $x = h$.
content.solution:
Each cross-sectional disk has radius $f(x) = (r/h)\,x$.
$$
 V &= \pi\defint{0}{h}\left(\frac{r}{h}\,x\right)^2\dx 
 = \pi\,\frac{r^2}{h^2}\defint{0}{h} x^2\dx \\
 &= \pi\,\frac{r^2}{h^2}\cdot\frac{h^3}{3} = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h.
$$
This confirms the classical formula: the volume of a cone is one-third the 
volume of the enclosing cylinder.

content.title: Volume of a sphere
content.problem:
Derive the volume of a sphere of radius $R$ by revolving a semicircle about 
the $x$-axis.
content.solution:
The semicircle $y = \sqrt{R^2 - x^2}$ extends from $x = -R$ to $x = R$. 
Revolving about the $x$-axis:
$$
 V &= \pi\defint{-R}{R}\left(\sqrt{R^2 - x^2}\right)^2\dx 
 = \pi\defint{-R}{R}(R^2 - x^2)\dx \\
 &= \pi\left[R^2 x - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-R}^{R} 
 = \pi\left[\left(R^3 - \frac{R^3}{3}\right) 
 - \left(-R^3 + \frac{R^3}{3}\right)\right] \\
 &= \pi\left[\frac{2R^3}{3} + \frac{2R^3}{3}\right] 
 = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3.
$$
The formula you memorized in geometry—derived from first principles.

content.text:
Archimedes (c.\,287–212 bc){The surface of any 
sphere is four times the greatest circle in it.}

content.title: Revolution of a specific curve
content.problem:
Find the volume of the solid obtained by revolving $y = \sqrt{x}$ about 
the $x$-axis on $[0, 4]$.

content.text: The Washer Method

When we revolve a region *between* two curves about an axis, the 
resulting solid has a hole through the middle—like a washer (or an 
annular ring). Each cross-sectional slice is a washer: a disk with a 
concentric disk removed.

**Step 1: Slice** perpendicular to the axis. Each slice is a washer 
with outer radius $R(x)$ and inner radius $r(x)$.

**Step 2: Approximate.** The area of a washer is 
$\pi R^2 - \pi r^2 = \pi(R^2 - r^2)$, so
\[
 \Delta V_k \approx \pi\bigl[R(x_k^*)^2 - r(x_k^*)^2\bigr]\,\Delta x.
\]
**Step 3: Sum** and **Step 4: Take the limit.**

content.title: Volume by Washers (Revolution about the $x$-axis)
content.content:
If $f(x) \ge g(x) \ge 0$ on $[a,b]$, the volume of the solid obtained by 
revolving the region between $y=f(x)$ and $y=g(x)$ about the $x$-axis is
\[
 V = \pi\defint{a}{b}\left\{\bigl[f(x)\bigr]^2 
 - \bigl[g(x)\bigr]^2\right\}\dx.
\]
Here $R(x) = f(x)$ (outer radius) and $r(x) = g(x)$ (inner radius).

content.caption: The washer method for volumes of revolution
content.alt: Diagram showing the washer method for computing volume when a region between two curves is revolved. The left panel shows two curves y = f(x) (upper) and y = g(x) (lower) with the region between them shaded. The right panel shows the resulting solid of revolution about the x-axis, which has a hollow center. A representative washer-shaped cross-section is highlighted, showing outer radius R = f(x), inner radius r = g(x), and thickness Δx. The washer has volume π(R² − r²)Δx.

content.title: Washer method --- basic
content.problem:
Find the volume of the solid obtained by revolving the region between 
$y = x^2$ and $y = \sqrt{x}$ about the $x$-axis.
content.solution:
The curves intersect where $x^2 = \sqrt{x}$, i.e., $x^4 = x$, so 
$x(x^3 - 1) = 0$, giving $x = 0, 1$.

On $[0,1]$, $\sqrt{x} \ge x^2$ (check $x = 1/4$: $1/2 > 1/16$). 
Thus $R(x) = \sqrt{x}$ (outer) and $r(x) = x^2$ (inner).
$$
 V &= \pi\defint{0}{1}\bigl[(\sqrt{x})^2 - (x^2)^2\bigr]\dx 
 = \pi\defint{0}{1}(x - x^4)\dx \\
 &= \pi\left[\frac{x^2}{2} - \frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^1 
 = \pi\left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{5}\right) = \frac{3\pi}{10}.
$$

content.title: Volume of the region between two parabolas
content.problem:
Find the volume when the region enclosed by $y = x$ and $y = x^2$ is 
revolved about the $x$-axis.
content.solution:
Intersection: $x = x^2$ gives $x = 0, 1$. On $[0,1]$, $x \ge x^2$, so 
$R(x) = x$ and $r(x) = x^2$.
$$
 V &= \pi\defint{0}{1}(x^2 - x^4)\dx 
 = \pi\left[\frac{x^3}{3} - \frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^1 
 = \pi\left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{5}\right) = \frac{2\pi}{15}.
$$

content.text: Revolution About the $y$-Axis

When we revolve about the $y$-axis, slices perpendicular to the axis of 
revolution are horizontal. We express the curve as $x = h(y)$ and 
integrate with respect to $y$.

content.title: Disks/Washers about the $y$-axis
content.content:
If the region between $x = f(y)$ and $x = g(y)$ for $c \le y \le d$ 
(with $f(y) \ge g(y) \ge 0$) is revolved about the $y$-axis, then
\[
 V = \pi\defint{c}{d}\left\{\bigl[f(y)\bigr]^2 
 - \bigl[g(y)\bigr]^2\right\}\dy.
\]
For a solid region with no hole, set $g(y) = 0$ (disk method in $y$).

content.title: Revolution about the $y$-axis
content.problem:
Find the volume of the solid obtained by revolving $y = x^2$ (i.e., 
$x = \sqrt{y}$) about the $y$-axis, from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$.
content.solution:
Each disk (perpendicular to the $y$-axis) has radius $x = \sqrt{y}$:
\[
 V = \pi\defint{0}{4}(\sqrt{y})^2\dy = \pi\defint{0}{4}y\dy 
 = \pi\left[\frac{y^2}{2}\right]_0^4 = 8\pi.
\]

content.text: Revolution About Other Horizontal and Vertical Lines

The key idea does not change when we revolve about a line other than a 
coordinate axis. The only difference is how we compute the *radius* 
of each disk or washer.

content.title: Adjusting Radii for Non-Axis Revolution
content.content:
When revolving about the line $y = L$:

- The radius to the curve $y = f(x)$ is $\abs{f(x) - L}$.

- The outer and inner radii become the distances from the curves to 
 the line $y = L$ (not to the $x$-axis).
The same principle applies for revolution about $x = L$, using distances 
to the line $x = L$.

content.title: Revolution about $y = 4$
content.problem:
Find the volume of the solid obtained by revolving the region bounded by 
$y = x^2$, $y = 0$, $x = 0$, and $x = 2$ about the line $y = 4$.
content.solution:
For each $x$ in $[0,2]$, the cross-section perpendicular to the $x$-axis 
is a washer:

- Outer radius: distance from $y = 0$ to $y = 4$: $R = 4$.

- Inner radius: distance from $y = x^2$ to $y = 4$: $r = 4 - x^2$.
$$
 V &= \pi\defint{0}{2}\bigl[4^2 - (4 - x^2)^2\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \pi\defint{0}{2}\bigl[16 - (16 - 8x^2 + x^4)\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \pi\defint{0}{2}(8x^2 - x^4)\dx \\
 &= \pi\left[\frac{8x^3}{3} - \frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^2 
 = \pi\left(\frac{64}{3} - \frac{32}{5}\right) 
 = \pi\cdot\frac{224}{15} = \frac{224\pi}{15}.
$$

content.title: Revolution about $y = -1$
content.problem:
Find the volume when the region between $y = \sqrt{x}$ and $y = x$ 
($0 \le x \le 1$) is revolved about the line $y = -1$.
content.solution:
On $[0,1]$, $\sqrt{x} \ge x$. Each curve's distance from $y = -1$ is 
shifted up by $1$:

- Outer radius: $R = \sqrt{x} - (-1) = \sqrt{x} + 1$.

- Inner radius: $r = x - (-1) = x + 1$.
$$
 V &= \pi\defint{0}{1}\bigl[(\sqrt{x}+1)^2 - (x+1)^2\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \pi\defint{0}{1}\bigl[(x + 2\sqrt{x} + 1) - (x^2 + 2x + 1)\bigr]\dx \\
 &= \pi\defint{0}{1}(x + 2\sqrt{x} - x^2 - 2x)\dx \\
 &= \pi\defint{0}{1}(2x^{1/2} - x - x^2)\dx \\
 &= \pi\left[\frac{4}{3}x^{3/2} - \frac{x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^1 
 = \pi\left(\frac{4}{3} - \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3}\right) 
 = \pi\cdot\frac{1}{2} = \frac{\pi}{2}.
$$

content.title: Revolution about a vertical line
content.problem:
Find the volume when the region bounded by $y = x^2$, $y = 0$, and $x = 2$ 
is revolved about the line $x = 3$.
content.solution:
We use horizontal slices (disks/washers in $y$). The region runs from 
$y = 0$ to $y = 4$. For a given $y$, $x$ ranges from $\sqrt{y}$ to $2$.

Revolving about $x = 3$:

- Outer radius: $R = 3 - \sqrt{y}$ (distance from $x=\sqrt{y}$ to $x=3$).

- Inner radius: $r = 3 - 2 = 1$ (distance from $x=2$ to $x=3$).
$$
 V &= \pi\defint{0}{4}\bigl[(3-\sqrt{y})^2 - 1\bigr]\dy \\
 &= \pi\defint{0}{4}(9 - 6\sqrt{y} + y - 1)\dy 
 = \pi\defint{0}{4}(8 - 6y^{1/2} + y)\dy \\
 &= \pi\left[8y - 4y^{3/2} + \frac{y^2}{2}\right]_0^4 
 = \pi(32 - 32 + 8) = 8\pi.
$$

content.text: A Note on General Cross-Sections

The disk and washer methods are special cases of a more general principle: 
if we know the area $A(x)$ of every cross-section of a solid perpendicular 
to the $x$-axis, then
\[
 V = \defint{a}{b} A(x)\dx.
\]

This is **Cavalieri's Principle** in 
integral form: the volume is the integral of cross-sectional area. Disks 
and washers arise when those cross-sections are circles; but the same idea 
works for squares, equilateral triangles, or any other known shape.

content.title: Square cross-sections
content.problem:
A solid has a circular base of radius $2$ (the disk $x^2 + y^2 \le 4$). 
Cross-sections perpendicular to the $x$-axis are squares. Find the volume.
content.solution:
At position $x$, the base chord has half-length $y = \sqrt{4-x^2}$, 
so the full side length of the square is $s = 2\sqrt{4-x^2}$. The 
cross-sectional area is $A(x) = s^2 = 4(4-x^2)$.
$$
 V &= \defint{-2}{2} 4(4-x^2)\dx = 8\defint{0}{2}(4-x^2)\dx 
 \quad\text{(even function)} \\
 &= 8\left[4x - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^2 
 = 8\left(8 - \frac{8}{3}\right) = 8\cdot\frac{16}{3} = \frac{128}{3}.
$$

content.title: Cavalieri and Indivisibles (1635)
content.content:
The Italian mathematician Bonaventura Cavalieri, a student of Galileo, 
published *Geometria indivisibilibus* in 1635, developing a method 
of "indivisibles" that treated solids as stacks of infinitely thin slices. 
Though not rigorous by modern standards, Cavalieri's insight—that volume 
equals the accumulation of cross-sectional areas—is precisely the 
principle that underlies our disk and washer formulas. His work paved the 
way for Newton and Leibniz a generation later.

marginNotes.text:
For this is what the Lord says—he who 
created the heavens, he is God; he who fashioned and made the earth, he 
founded it.

I was there when he set the heavens in 
place, when he marked out the horizon on the face of the deep.

The earth is the Lord's, and everything in it, 
the world, and all who live in it.

marginNotes.author: Johannes Kepler (1571--1630)
marginNotes.text:
Geometry has two great treasures: 
one is the theorem of Pythagoras, the other the division of a line into 
extreme and mean ratio.

marginNotes.author: Bonaventura Cavalieri (1598--1647)
marginNotes.text:
If two solids have equal 
cross-sectional areas at every height, they have equal volumes.
